title: Endpoints & Bindings
overview: Clarifying how abstract operations in a WSDL are mapped to physical network protocols and URLs.
whyItMatters: When configuring SOAP test tools, you often have to choose a binding and an endpoint. Understanding these WSDL concepts ensures you send the right message format to the right address.
explanation:
In a WSDL document, the architecture is split into the **abstract** definition (what the service does) and the **concrete** definition (how and where to access it). Bindings and Endpoints belong to the concrete definition.

### Bindings (`<binding>`)
The `<binding>` element maps the abstract operations (defined in the `<portType>`) to a specific data format and communication protocol.
- **Protocol**: It specifies the transport mechanism. The vast majority of the time, this is SOAP over HTTP (`http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/http`), but SOAP can also be bound to SMTP (email) or JMS (message queues).
- **Style**: It specifies how the XML payload is structured. 
  - *Document/Literal*: The payload is a standard XML document validated against an XSD schema. (Most common modern approach).
  - *RPC/Encoded*: The payload is formatted to look like a Remote Procedure Call (passing function arguments). (Legacy approach).

### Endpoints / Ports (`<port>` and `<service>`)
The `<service>` element groups together a set of related ports. 
A `<port>` (often referred to as the **Endpoint**) specifies the physical network address where the bound service is hosted.
- It contains the `<soap:address>` tag, which provides the actual URL (e.g., `http://api.bank.com/paymentservice`).

### Why separate them?
Separating the abstract operations from the bindings allows the same service to be exposed over multiple protocols simultaneously. A company could expose `GetAccountBalance` over HTTP for web clients, and over JMS for internal asynchronous processing, using the exact same abstract `<portType>`.
keyPoints: A Binding maps abstract operations to a concrete protocol (like HTTP) and data style.
An Endpoint (Port) provides the physical network address (URL) to access the service.
Separating bindings from operations allows a single service to be exposed over multiple transport protocols.
codeExample:
<!-- Abstract Operations -->
<portType name="PaymentOperations">
  <operation name="ProcessPayment">
    <!-- inputs/outputs -->
  </operation>
</portType>

<!-- Concrete Binding: Mapping to SOAP over HTTP -->
<binding name="PaymentHttpBinding" type="tns:PaymentOperations">
  <soap:binding style="document" transport="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/http"/>
  <operation name="ProcessPayment">
    <soap:operation soapAction="http://example.com/ProcessPayment"/>
  </operation>
</binding>

<!-- Concrete Endpoint: The actual URL -->
<service name="PaymentService">
  <port name="PaymentPort" binding="tns:PaymentHttpBinding">
    <soap:address location="https://api.example.com/v1/payments"/>
  </port>
</service>
realWorldExample: You are given a WSDL for an internal shipping calculator. The WSDL defines one `<portType>` but contains two `<port>` elements inside the `<service>`: one bound to HTTP for synchronous web requests, and another bound to JMS for asynchronous internal queue processing. Depending on your test scenario, you select the appropriate endpoint URL/queue address.
commonMistakes: Confusing the namespace URIs found throughout the WSDL with the actual endpoint URL. Only the URL inside `<soap:address>` is the destination for network requests.
Attempting to send an RPC-style payload to a service bound as Document/Literal, causing XML parsing errors on the server.
bestPractices: When automating SOAP tests, extract the endpoint URL dynamically from the WSDL if the environment (dev/qa/prod) changes, rather than hardcoding it.
Ensure the `SOAPAction` HTTP header in your request matches the `soapAction` defined in the WSDL `<binding>` block.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between a portType and a binding in WSDL?
interviewQuestions.a: A portType is abstract; it defines the operations (methods) and the messages (parameters) they use, similar to a Java Interface. A binding is concrete; it dictates how those abstract operations are actually transmitted over the wire, such as specifying SOAP over HTTP and the XML serialization style.

revisionSummary: Bindings map abstract WSDL operations to concrete protocols (e.g., SOAP over HTTP). Endpoints (Ports) specify the physical network URL where the bound service is hosted.
